refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from the combined design of propulsion, control, and operational strategies, not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a data interface (telemetry, dashboard, or log) that reports: (a) total energy consumed (kWh or equivalent) over a defined evaluation period, and (b) total work output (tons moved) over the same period, with sufficient granularity to compute energy_per_ton. Confirm that reported values are traceable to onboard sensors and that the calculation method is documented and auditable.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and record total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure and record total work output (mass moved) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and report the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) at defined intervals or on demand.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.

DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the primary equipment (e.g., engine, drivetrain, load cell); stream dual measurements to a central aggregator that computes and reports energy_per_ton continuously.

DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: one channel measures cumulative energy consumption via fuel consumption or power draw; a second channel measures cumulative work output via distance, load, or cycle counting; both feed into a unified data pipeline that computes energy_per_ton at configurable intervals.

DP4: Decompose measurement into three independent logical subsystems: (A) energy quantification subsystem (measures input energy), (B) work quantification subsystem (measures output tonnage), (C) metric computation and reporting subsystem (consumes A and B outputs, computes and reports energy_per_ton); each subsystem has independent sensor selection, calibration, and data flow.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Decompose measurement into three independent logical subsystems: (A) energy quantification subsystem (measures input energy), (B) work quantification subsystem (measures output tonnage), (C) metric computation and reporting subsystem (consumes A and B outputs, computes and reports energy_per_ton); each subsystem has independent sensor selection, calibration, and data flow.

potential_sources: Temporal synchronization: energy and work measurements must be time-aligned to ensure valid energy_per_ton ratios; mitigation: explicit timestamp requirement in subsystem interface contract.
Calibration drift: independent sensors may drift at different rates; mitigation: define independent calibration schedules and acceptance criteria per subsystem.
Data availability: if either energy or work measurement fails, energy_per_ton becomes undefined; mitigation: define fallback logic and reporting rules in the metric computation subsystem.
Unit consistency: energy and work must use consistent units (e.g., kWh and metric tons); mitigation: enforce unit conversion and validation in the metric computation subsystem.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves low coupling by isolating measurement concerns (A, B) from computation (C). Each subsystem can be designed, tested, and evolved independently. Coupling is limited to well-defined data interfaces (timestamps, units, availability flags) rather than shared hardware or algorithms. This supports modular implementation and reduces risk of cascading failures.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is measurable via a single, well-defined input channel (e.g., fuel flow, electrical power draw). RISK: Hybrid or multi-source systems (e.g., engine + battery) may require subsystem A to aggregate multiple energy streams; mitigation: clarify energy source topology early.

ASSUMPTION: Work output is quantifiable as tonnage moved. RISK: If work definition is ambiguous (e.g., partial loads, vertical vs. horizontal movement), subsystem B may require domain-specific logic; mitigation: align work definition with operational policy and acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Measurement infrastructure (sensors, data logging, communication) is available and reliable. RISK: Harsh worksite conditions may degrade sensor accuracy or data transmission; mitigation: specify environmental tolerance and redundancy requirements for each subsystem.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and establish statistical confidence in energy_per_ton. RISK: Short evaluation windows may not capture representative operating conditions; mitigation: define minimum evaluation period and sampling frequency.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of measurement equipment without introducing new hazards. RISK: Sensor placement or wiring may conflict with safety protocols; mitigation: coordinate with safety stakeholder early in IM design phase.

ASSUMPTION: Energy_per_ton target E_target is achievable with the selected equipment and operational practices. RISK: If target is unrealistic, measurement will confirm non-compliance but not enable improvement; mitigation: validate E_target against baseline performance data before commitment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) definition of 'work output' (e.g., metric tons of material moved horizontally on level ground, excluding vertical lift), (2) energy source topology (single or multiple inputs), (3) required measurement accuracy and confidence level (e.g., ±5% over 95% of evaluation period), (4) evaluation period duration and sampling frequency, (5) data retention and reporting format requirements. This will reduce ambiguity in subsystem A and B design and enable clearer acceptance criteria for DP4 implementation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement and energy efficiency analytics platform that integrates energy quantification, work quantification, and metric computation subsystems with unified sensor management, calibration orchestration, and reporting infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw sensor data (energy input, tonnage output) from Vehicle; transmits computed energy_per_ton metrics and subsystem health status.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives measurement configuration requests (sensor selection, calibration parameters, reporting intervals) from operational control.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits alerts if energy or work quantification subsystems detect anomalies or calibration drift that could affect safety-critical decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits aggregated energy efficiency reports and analytics to external systems or cloud infrastructure.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP4 describes a measurement and analytics architecture with three logical subsystems (energy, work, metrics). NGHE Solution is explicitly positioned as the analytics/efficiency platform. All three subsystems (A, B, C) naturally reside within NGHE: sensor selection, calibration, and data flow orchestration are core NGHE responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (Vehicle interface) and reporting (Network Link) cross boundary.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the data source (sensors), not the measurement logic. Placing DP4's three subsystems in Vehicle would couple measurement logic tightly to vehicle hardware, violate separation of concerns, and force Vehicle to own calibration and metric computation—responsibilities that belong in analytics layer. High diffusion: energy and work quantification would leak into vehicle control logic.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a measurement platform. Placing energy quantification, work quantification, and metric computation subsystems in Teleoperation would create severe architectural confusion and tight coupling between operator commands and measurement logic. Subsystems would diffuse across Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link.

Weak fit. Safety Services may consume energy_per_ton metrics for safety decisions (e.g., energy anomalies as fault indicators), but should not own the measurement subsystems themselves. DP4 describes measurement architecture, not safety logic. Placing energy and work quantification in Safety Services would blur safety vs. analytics responsibilities and force Safety Services to manage sensor calibration—outside its domain.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP4 (three independent measurement subsystems with unified sensor selection, calibration, and data flow) is fundamentally an analytics and efficiency measurement architecture. NGHE Solution is the designated platform for energy efficiency analytics and is the only module with sufficient scope and responsibility to contain all three subsystems (energy quantification, work quantification, metric computation) without diffusion. Vehicle provides raw sensor data (Telemetry in); NGHE processes and reports (Telemetry out, Network Link out). Safety Services consumes alerts (SafetyAlert out) if anomalies are detected.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle sensor hardware changes (e.g., new energy sensor type), NGHE must adapt sensor selection logic. Mitigation: Define clear Telemetry interface contract so Vehicle changes do not force NGHE subsystem redesign.

Risk: If multiple external systems require energy_per_ton metrics, NGHE reporting subsystem (C) may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Design metric computation subsystem (C) with pluggable output adapters (e.g., REST API, message queue) to avoid tight coupling to specific consumers.

Risk: Calibration orchestration (part of DP4) may require real-time feedback from Vehicle sensors during calibration runs. Mitigation: Establish bidirectional Telemetry interface with explicit calibration mode signaling to avoid ambiguity between normal operation and calibration data.

Risk: Safety Services may need to trigger emergency measurement shutdown if anomalies are detected. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as bidirectional or add explicit control signal from Safety Services to NGHE to pause measurement subsystems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. FR acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable via DP4's three subsystems: (A) energy quantification provides numerator, (B) work quantification provides denominator, (C) metric computation produces the ratio. NGHE Solution IM boundary contains all three subsystems and owns the measurement pipeline end-to-end. Telemetry interface (inbound) receives raw sensor data from Vehicle; Telemetry interface (outbound) and Network Link transmit computed metrics for validation against E_target. Traceability chain is clear: FR → DP4 (logical decomposition) → NGHE Solution IM (realization packaging).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. DP4 allocation to NGHE Solution is unambiguous. NGHE owns sensor selection, calibration orchestration, and data flow for all three subsystems. Vehicle is a data source (sensor provider), not a measurement logic owner. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert outputs but does not own measurement subsystems. Teleoperation is excluded from measurement responsibility. Ownership boundaries are crisp and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly and sufficiently addresses FR. The three-subsystem decomposition (energy quantification, work quantification, metric computation) is logically sound and maps cleanly to the FR requirement: 'enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.' Each subsystem has independent sensor selection and calibration, reducing coupling and improving modularity. Data flow is explicit: A and B feed C, which produces the required metric. DP4 is implementable, testable, and traceable to acceptance criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).

diffusion_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface (inbound) is a bridge point. If Vehicle sensor hardware or data format changes, NGHE sensor selection logic (subsystem A and B) must adapt. Mitigation: Establish a versioned Telemetry contract with explicit schema and sensor metadata (e.g., sensor type, calibration state, timestamp precision) to decouple Vehicle hardware evolution from NGHE subsystem logic.

Risk: Calibration orchestration (part of DP4) may require bidirectional signaling with Vehicle during calibration runs (e.g., 'enter calibration mode', 'apply known load'). Current Telemetry interface is described as 'inout' but lacks explicit calibration mode semantics. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface documentation to define calibration mode signaling and distinguish calibration data from operational data.

Risk: Metric computation subsystem (C) may become a diffusion point if multiple external consumers (e.g., Safety Services, Network Link, Tasking feedback) require different metric formats or update frequencies. Mitigation: Design subsystem C with pluggable output adapters (e.g., REST API, message queue, direct Safety Services feed) to avoid tight coupling and ensure subsystem C logic remains centralized.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface (outbound) is unidirectional. If Safety Services detects a critical anomaly and needs to pause or reset measurement subsystems, NGHE has no inbound control signal. Mitigation: Consider adding a bidirectional SafetyAlert interface or explicit 'MeasurementControl' interface from Safety Services to NGHE to enable emergency shutdown or recalibration requests.

Risk: Tasking interface (inbound) receives 'measurement configuration requests' but scope is vague. If Tasking can dynamically change sensor selection or calibration parameters during operation, subsystem A and B logic may diffuse across multiple configuration sources. Mitigation: Define Tasking interface contract to specify which parameters are mutable at runtime vs. static, and establish a configuration versioning scheme to track subsystem state.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. DP4 is fully contained within NGHE Solution IM boundary (containment_score: 95, diffusion_score: 5). All three logical subsystems (energy quantification, work quantification, metric computation) reside in NGHE with unified sensor management and calibration orchestration. Vehicle is correctly positioned as a data source (Telemetry inbound), not a measurement logic owner. Safety Services and Teleoperation are correctly excluded from measurement subsystem ownership. The only diffusion points are external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link), which are expected and manageable via interface contracts. No evidence of scattered DP responsibilities across unintended modules.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize Telemetry interface contract. Document sensor metadata schema (sensor type, calibration state, timestamp precision, data quality flags) to enable NGHE subsystems A and B to adapt to Vehicle sensor changes without redesign. Include version negotiation mechanism.

Revision 2: Extend Telemetry interface with explicit calibration mode signaling. Define state machine (e.g., 'operational', 'calibration_in_progress', 'calibration_complete') to distinguish calibration data from operational data and enable bidirectional coordination during calibration runs.

Revision 3: Design metric computation subsystem (C) with pluggable output adapters. Specify adapter interface (e.g., 'MetricConsumer') to allow Safety Services, Network Link, and future consumers to subscribe to energy_per_ton metrics without forcing subsystem C to know about each consumer. Centralize metric computation logic in NGHE; distribute consumption logic to consumers.

Revision 4: Add bidirectional SafetyAlert interface or explicit MeasurementControl interface. Enable Safety Services to request measurement subsystem pause, reset, or recalibration in response to detected anomalies. Define control semantics (e.g., 'pause_measurement', 'reset_calibration', 'emergency_shutdown') and response acknowledgment.

Revision 5: Clarify Tasking interface scope. Document which measurement configuration parameters (sensor selection, calibration parameters, reporting intervals) are mutable at runtime vs. static. Establish configuration versioning and audit trail to track subsystem state changes and ensure traceability for acceptance criterion validation.

Revision 6: Define acceptance criterion validation procedure. Specify how NGHE Solution will collect, aggregate, and report energy_per_ton measurements over the agreed evaluation period to enable comparison against E_target. Include data quality checks, outlier handling, and reporting format.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and DP4 is logically sound and well-contained within NGHE Solution IM. However, the gate analysis identifies six critical diffusion risks and interface contract gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Given the high safety criticality of this measurement system (energy_per_ton feeds into safety-critical decisions via SafetyAlert), proceeding to lower-level decomposition without formalizing these interface contracts and control semantics risks propagating ambiguity into subsystem-level designs. The recommended revisions are architectural (interface contracts, bidirectional signaling, configuration versioning) rather than functional (FR or DP logic), and must be captured at the current level to ensure subsystems A, B, and C are designed with correct assumptions about external coordination and data quality. REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL is the appropriate decision to harden DP4 and IM boundaries before decomposing into sub-FRs for subsystems A, B, and C.

revise.why: DP4 is logically correct but requires explicit architectural refinement to address interface contract gaps and bidirectional control semantics. Specifically: (1) Telemetry interface contract must formalize sensor metadata schema and version negotiation to decouple Vehicle hardware evolution from NGHE subsystem logic. (2) Calibration mode signaling must be added to Telemetry to enable bidirectional coordination during calibration runs. (3) Metric computation subsystem (C) must be designed with pluggable output adapters to prevent diffusion across multiple consumer formats. (4) SafetyAlert interface must be extended to bidirectional or supplemented with explicit MeasurementControl interface to enable Safety Services to request measurement subsystem pause/reset. (5) Tasking interface scope must be clarified to distinguish mutable runtime parameters from static configuration, with versioning and audit trail. (6) Acceptance criterion validation procedure must be defined to specify how energy_per_ton measurements are collected, aggregated, and reported for comparison against E_target. These refinements are DP-level (design logic and interface specifications) and must be resolved before sub-FR decomposition to ensure subsystems A, B, and C inherit correct assumptions about external coordination, data quality, and control semantics.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Vehicle Telemetry interface will remain stable in sensor type, data format, and timestamp precision across the evaluation period. If Vehicle hardware or firmware changes, NGHE subsystems A and B must adapt without redesign.

Assumption 2: Calibration runs can be coordinated via explicit mode signaling (e.g., 'enter calibration mode', 'apply known load') between NGHE and Vehicle. Current Telemetry interface is described as 'inout' but lacks explicit calibration state machine semantics.

Assumption 3: Metric computation subsystem (C) will be consumed by multiple external systems (Safety Services, Network Link, Tasking feedback) with potentially different format and frequency requirements. Subsystem C logic must remain centralized in NGHE; consumption logic must be distributed to consumers via adapters.

Assumption 4: Safety Services can detect measurement anomalies and may need to request measurement subsystem pause, reset, or recalibration. Current SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (NGHE → Safety Services); bidirectional control is required.

Assumption 5: Tasking interface can request measurement configuration changes (sensor selection, calibration parameters, reporting intervals) at runtime. Scope and mutability constraints are currently vague and must be formalized to prevent diffusion of configuration logic.

Assumption 6: Energy_per_ton acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) will be validated by comparing aggregated measurements over an agreed evaluation period. Data quality checks, outlier handling, and reporting format are not yet specified.
open_risks: Risk 1 (HIGH): Telemetry interface contract is incomplete. If Vehicle sensor hardware or data format changes, NGHE subsystems A and B may require redesign. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry contract with versioned sensor metadata schema and version negotiation mechanism before sub-FR decomposition.

Risk 2 (HIGH): Calibration mode signaling is undefined. Bidirectional coordination between NGHE and Vehicle during calibration runs is not explicitly specified in Telemetry interface. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface with explicit calibration state machine (operational, calibration_in_progress, calibration_complete) before sub-FR decomposition.

Risk 3 (MEDIUM): Metric computation subsystem (C) may become a diffusion point if multiple external consumers require different formats or frequencies. Mitigation: Design subsystem C with pluggable output adapters (MetricConsumer interface) before sub-FR decomposition to centralize metric logic and distribute consumption.

Risk 4 (HIGH): SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional. Safety Services cannot request measurement subsystem pause, reset, or recalibration in response to detected anomalies. Mitigation: Add bidirectional SafetyAlert interface or explicit MeasurementControl interface before sub-FR decomposition to enable emergency control.

Risk 5 (MEDIUM): Tasking interface scope is vague. Runtime mutability of measurement configuration parameters (sensor selection, calibration parameters, reporting intervals) is not constrained. Mitigation: Clarify Tasking interface contract with configuration versioning and audit trail before sub-FR decomposition.

Risk 6 (HIGH): Acceptance criterion validation procedure is not defined. How NGHE will collect, aggregate, and report energy_per_ton measurements for comparison against E_target is unspecified. Mitigation: Define validation procedure with data quality checks, outlier handling, and reporting format before sub-FR decomposition.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Formalized Telemetry interface contract document specifying sensor metadata schema (sensor type, calibration state, timestamp precision, data quality flags), version negotiation mechanism, and backward compatibility strategy.

Evidence 2: Extended Telemetry interface specification with explicit calibration mode signaling, including state machine definition (operational, calibration_in_progress, calibration_complete) and bidirectional coordination semantics.

Evidence 3: Metric computation subsystem (C) design document specifying pluggable output adapter interface (MetricConsumer), adapter registration mechanism, and examples of adapters for Safety Services, Network Link, and Tasking feedback.

Evidence 4: SafetyAlert interface extension or new MeasurementControl interface specification defining bidirectional control semantics (pause_measurement, reset_calibration, emergency_shutdown), request/response protocol, and acknowledgment handling.

Evidence 5: Tasking interface contract clarifying which measurement configuration parameters are mutable at runtime vs. static, configuration versioning scheme, and audit trail mechanism for tracking subsystem state changes.

Evidence 6: Acceptance criterion validation procedure document specifying data collection methodology, aggregation logic, outlier handling strategy, data quality checks, reporting format, and comparison procedure against E_target threshold.
